title: A Beautiful Day in Beacon
summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program opens the Civil Rights History Project interview with Pete Seeger at his home in Beacon, New York, in July 2011, and asks him to recall the September 1949 Paul Robeson concert at Peekskill, where Seeger opened with a few songs.

title: Wake Up America, Peekskill Did
summary: Seeger describes the two 1949 attacks on the Robeson concert as a near-miss with fascism, recounting J. Edgar Hoover's prepared detention camps, Truman's official giving the American Legion a green light, police trucking teenagers to gather throwing stones, the Ku Klux Klan and Lake Valhalla German Americans, and pre-printed hate signs that echoed Kristallnacht before the backlash turned public opinion.

title: No Anniversary Without Music
summary: Seeger recalls Alan Lomax telling him about Highlander Folk School, the 1940 photograph of him with Woody Guthrie, and being called by Myles Horton after Zilphia Horton's death to sing at the school's 25th anniversary in 1957, where he first met King, Abernathy, and Rosa Parks and was photographed before the barn after King spoke.

title: He's Here, He's Here
summary: Seeger describes the only other time he met King, a year before the assassination, singing at a massive United Nations peace rally the day after King's Riverside Church Beyond Vietnam speech, watching six men hold back an adulatory crowd around King's car, and hearing King call his country the greatest purveyor of violence, with a speculative aside about Lyndon Johnson's fury and a call to Hoover.

title: If We Are Nonviolent
summary: Seeger tells how a two-inch New York Times item led him to the Montgomery Improvement Association and E.D. Nixon, who explained calling King to host the protest meeting and arranging it before the deacons even approved, and how King insisted the boycott would be won only through nonviolence, mediating between angry young people and cautious professionals until they reached a compromise.

title: Stay Out of the Way of Bull Connor
summary: Seeger recounts learning the name Eugene Bull Connor while hitchhiking through the South in 1940 and staying with Joe Gelders, who had been badly beaten, and argues that Connor's use of fire hoses and dogs against children opened the country's eyes to how terrible the segregation system was.

title: You Don't Have the Song Right
summary: Seeger explains that Zilphia Horton taught him We Shall Overcome and he printed it imperfectly in People's Songs, but Guy Carawan and Frank Hamilton made it spread by setting it in 12/8 time learned from gospel singing at a Baptist church, underscoring how rhythm is essential to African American music.

title: Cross Your Hands
topic: SNCC and Singing in the Movement
summary: Seeger describes Guy Carawan's 1960 Singing in the Movement weekend at Highlander, drawing fifty or sixty young people from Texas to Florida, where We Shall Overcome became the hit, and how weeks later at the Raleigh founding of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, Carawan taught the crowd the crossed-hands, body-swaying way of singing rooted in African tradition.

part: Carnegie Hall and the Wider World
title: Answer Back in the Harmony
summary: Seeger recalls a Carnegie Hall concert before taking his family on a 35-country world tour, including Toshi's father reuniting with his Japanese family after fifty years, and bringing Black and white neighbors from Beacon to sit on either side of him and learn the call-and-response harmony so they would get the audience singing.

title: From Spirituals to Swing
summary: Seeger describes the hundred-thousand-dollar union stage-crew cost of recording at Carnegie covered by Columbia, and John Hammond Jr., great-grandson of Cornelius Vanderbilt, who discovered Brownie McGhee and Sonny Terry and staged the 1938 From Spirituals to Swing concert that Seeger attended in his last weeks at Harvard.

title: Just Like the Fisk Jubilee Singers
summary: Seeger recounts performing in Albany, Georgia in 1962 to a tepid response when he sang English ballads, meeting Bernice Johnson Reagon and Cordell Reagon, and suggesting to the SNCC office in Atlanta that singers could raise money traveling like the Fisk Jubilee Singers, leading to the Freedom Singers whose first tour Toshi set up from her list of college contacts.

title: They Found the Bodies
summary: Seeger tells of flying to Jackson with William Kunstler in the summer of 1964, a hostile passenger threatening him outside the airport, singing in Hattiesburg and Meridian, and announcing to two hundred people in a church that the bodies of Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney had been found, then singing Fred Hellerman's Healing River in the silence.

title: What Do We Want? Freedom!
summary: Seeger recalls the telegram from King inviting him and Toshi to the Selma to Montgomery march, joining some three hundred marchers in the rain with helicopters watching the cornfields for snipers, Harry Belafonte urging them on, and first hearing the new call-and-response of We Shall Overcome answered with shouts of Freedom, before a break and a song about the river by Storm King Mountain.

title: Pick Up the Torch
summary: Seeger reflects that King brought nonviolence, anti-war, environment, social justice, racial equality, and women's concerns together as one broad arena, and that knowing he would be assassinated King used his final speech to tell people to pick up the torch, with Seeger giving humanity a fifty-fifty chance of surviving the century.

title: The Spirit of Beacon Day
summary: Seeger describes a widowed Beacon woman who, after a high school race riot, gathered other women to close Main Street one day a year for the Spirit of Beacon Day, inviting churches, civic clubs, and the Beacon Sloop Club, an event that grew until two-thirds of the town turned out for a parade, dancing, and music from saris to the River Town Kids' new songs.

title: Don't Try to Get Reelected
summary: Asked about the first Black president amid a long war, Seeger says he expected compromises but not so many, and regrets that in his ten seconds with Obama, who told him his mother played his records, he did not advise the president to do the best job he could rather than aim to get reelected in 2012.

title: A Government of Corporations
topic: Rutherford B. Hayes and the Courts
summary: Seeger tells the story of Rutherford B. Hayes, whom he calls the worst president for withdrawing federal troops from the South after the Civil War in a deadlocked election, and the later Santa Clara County v. Southern Pacific Railroad ruling that ended corporate charter revocation, prompting Hayes to declare it a government of corporations rather than of the people.

title: The Cow Was the Union Organizer
summary: Seeger recalls the radical Jewish garment workers' Camp Nitgedaiget near Beacon and the puppet troupe he built in the 1930s, performing a skit during the New York Dairy Farmers Union strike in which a cow advises an underpaid farmer to organize with the others, with Seeger playing the cow as they passed the hat at strike meetings.

title: They Knew They Could Be Killed
topic: Fannie Lou Hamer and Medgar Evers
summary: Seeger speaks of Stacey White's annual gathering in Mississippi remembering Fannie Lou Hamer, who was beaten so badly she later died of her injuries, the heroism it took for Medgar Evers and the three slain workers who knew assassins were coming, and an FBI agent present who did nothing, before the interview closes with thanks and the Library of Congress and Smithsonian sign-off.
